summary:
The Meiji Constitution, promulgated on 11 February 1889 and coming into force in 1890, established the constitutional framework for the Japanese state created by the [[event:2544|Meiji Restoration]]. It was Japan's first written constitution and one of the earliest constitutional documents in Asia, creating a bicameral parliament (the Imperial Diet) while preserving the emperor's sovereign authority and ensuring the oligarchs retained dominant political control.

The constitution was drafted primarily by Itō Hirobumi, who had traveled to Europe to study constitutional models. He and his colleagues chose to model it primarily on the Prussian/German imperial constitution rather than the more liberal British or French models, preferring a system that balanced representative institutions with strong executive power concentrated in the imperial institution and the genrō (elder statesmen). The emperor was described as 'sacred and inviolable' and retained extensive powers including supreme command of the armed forces, the power to declare war and peace, and the authority to dissolve the Diet.

The constitution established a bicameral legislature: an elected House of Representatives and an appointive House of Peers. The franchise was initially limited to men paying a certain level of taxes, representing about 1 percent of the population, though it was expanded progressively. Cabinet ministers were responsible to the emperor rather than to the parliament, limiting parliamentary power in ways that enabled the military in subsequent decades to exert independent political influence by directly advising the emperor.

The Meiji Constitution remained in force until 1947, when the post-World War II American occupation imposed the current pacifist constitution. Its sixty-year tenure spanned Japan's transformation into an industrial and military power, its period of imperial expansion, and its catastrophic defeat in the Second World War.
